chunk_summary: This section outlines a clinic workflow for assessing and managing vegetarian diets in children, focusing on growth, protein intake, and micronutrient risks, with practical follow-up strategies and red flag identification.
learning_objectives: Understand how to classify vegetarian dietary patterns and assess their impact on growth and nutrition.
Learn to identify and address high-risk micronutrient deficiencies in vegetarian children.
Develop practical follow-up plans based on dietary history and clinical red flags.
key_facts: Classify vegetarian patterns as lacto-vegetarian, lacto-ovo vegetarian, or vegan, and assess daily versus periodic restrictions (e.g., fasting days).
Anchor assessments in three outcomes: growth trajectory, daily protein intake, and exposure to high-risk micronutrients (e.g., vitamin B12, iron, calcium, vitamin D, zinc, iodine).
Excess milk intake in toddlers displaces iron- and protein-rich foods, increasing the risk of iron deficiency anemia (IDA).
For infants, watery meals like 'dal water/rice water' are a common failure mode and should be avoided.
Red flags requiring urgent referral include lethargy, syncope, seizures, severe pallor, neuroregression, and edema.
reference_quote: Excess milk displaces iron- and protein-containing foods and increases IDA risk; plan an explicit cap when intake is high. (Source: IAP Parental Guideline: Complementary Feeding)
document_key_topics: Nutritional assessment and follow-up in children
High-risk micronutrient deficiencies and red flags
